content:
# 2 Timothy Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "by his appearing and his kingdom" vs. "when he comes to set up his Kingdom"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and by his appearing and his kingdom" | kai tēn epiphaneian autou kai tēn basileian autou |
| LOGOS | "and by his appearing and his kingdom" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "who will someday judge the living and the dead when he comes to set up his Kingdom" | Restructures; adds "someday," "when he comes to set up" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul adjures Timothy "by his appearing and his kingdom" — two nouns joined by kai, the basis of the solemn charge. PNEUMA restructures into a temporal clause ("when he comes to set up"), adding a narrative arc not in the Greek. Paul's charge rests on the fact of the appearing and the kingdom, not their timing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA restructures and adds temporal framing.

---

### v.6 — "being poured out" vs. "being poured out as a drink offering" vs. "being poured out as an offering to God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am already being poured out" | spendomai — sacrificial term |
| LOGOS | "I am already being poured out as a drink offering" | Adds "as a drink offering" — implicit in spendomai |
| PNEUMA | "my life is already being poured out as an offering to God" | Adds "my life," "to God"; generalizes to "offering" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. spendomai specifically means "to be poured out as a drink offering" — the libation metaphor is built into the verb. LOGOS makes this explicit. PNEUMA generalizes to "offering" (losing the specific drink-offering image) and adds "my life" and "to God" — Paul says "I am being poured out," using his person as the subject.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA generalizes and adds.

---

### v.8 — "in that day" vs. "on the day of his return"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in that day" | en ekeinē tē hēmera |
| LOGOS | "on that day" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "on the day of his return" | Specifies "that day" as "the day of his return" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul says "on that day" (ekeinē tē hēmera) — a loaded eschatological phrase that early Christians understood without specification. PNEUMA identifies "that day" as "the day of his return" — a correct interpretation, but Paul leaves the phrase unspecified, trusting Timothy to understand.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA specifies what Paul leaves implied.

---

### v.10 — "having loved the present age" vs. "he loves the things of this life"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having loved the present age" | agapēsas ton nyn aiōna |
| LOGOS | "in love with this present world" | Same — "world" for aiōn |
| PNEUMA | "he loves the things of this life" | "Things of this life" for ton nyn aiōna |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. agapēsas ton nyn aiōna — Demas loved "the present age/world." PNEUMA changes to "the things of this life" — fragmenting the singular concept (the age) into plural things. Paul's point is that Demas chose this age over the coming one — a cosmic choice, not a preference for material goods.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA fragments "the present age" into "things."

---

### v.14 — "the Lord will repay him" vs. "the Lord will judge him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Lord will repay him according to his works" | apodōsei autō ho kyrios kata ta erga autou |
| LOGOS | "the Lord will repay him according to his deeds" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the Lord will judge him for what he has done" | "Judge" for apodōsei ("will repay/render") |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. apodidōmi means "to give back / repay / render" — it is retributive language, not judicial. Paul says the Lord will repay Alexander, not merely judge him. "Judge" (PNEUMA) introduces a courtroom metaphor; Paul uses a marketplace/debt metaphor — rendering to each according to his works (echoing Psalm 62:12, Proverbs 24:12).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes metaphor from repayment to judgment.

---

### v.17 — "the mouth of the lion" vs. "certain death"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion" | ek stomatos leontos |
| LOGOS | "I was rescued from the lion's mouth" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he rescued me from certain death" | Interprets "lion's mouth" as "certain death" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "The mouth of the lion" (stomatos leontos) echoes Psalm 22:21 — a deliberate allusion. Whether Paul means literal danger (the arena), Nero, Satan, or death itself, the biblical allusion is the point. PNEUMA's "certain death" removes the allusion and flattens the metaphor to a single interpretation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA removes the biblical allusion.

---

### v.19 — "Prisca" vs. "Priscilla"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Greet Prisca and Aquila" | Priskan — formal name |
| LOGOS | "Greet Prisca and Aquila" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Give my greetings to Priscilla and Aquila" | "Priscilla" — diminutive form (used in Acts) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Sinaiticus has Priskan (Prisca) — the formal name. "Priscilla" is the diminutive form used by Luke in Acts. Paul consistently uses the formal "Prisca" in his letters (Rom 16:3, 1 Cor 16:19). PNEUMA substitutes the more familiar diminutive.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA uses diminutive form.

---

### v.22 — "Grace be with you" — singular or plural?

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Grace be with you" | hē charis meth' hymōn — plural "you" |
| LOGOS | "Grace be with you" | Same — "you" ambiguous in English |
| PNEUMA | "And may his grace be with all of you" | Adds "his," "all of"; makes plural explicit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek hymōn is plural — the final greeting extends beyond Timothy to the community. PNEUMA adds "his" (not in Greek) and "all of" to make the plural explicit. English "you" is already ambiguous; the Greek is clear.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "his" and "all of."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 3 | "Someday/when he comes to set up" (v.1), "my life/to God" (v.6), "his/all of" (v.22) |
| PNEUMA specifies what Paul implies | 2 | "Day of his return" for "that day" (v.8), "certain death" for "lion's mouth" (v.17) |
| PNEUMA changes metaphors | 2 | "Judge" for "repay" (v.14), "things of this life" for "present age" (v.10) |
| PNEUMA substitutes names | 1 | "Priscilla" for "Prisca" (v.19) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 4's most notable disagreement is v.17 — PNEUMA's removal of "the lion's mouth," which eliminates the deliberate allusion to Psalm 22:21. Paul, facing death, echoes the psalm of the righteous sufferer — a connection that resonates deeply with his situation and with the psalm's messianic overtones. PNEUMA also changes the metaphor in v.14 from repayment to judgment, and specifies "that day" (v.8) where Paul trusts the reader to understand. The v.19 Prisca/Priscilla variant, while minor, illustrates PNEUMA's tendency to substitute the more familiar form over the textual one.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
